1: vaani bishnat achat leDaryavesh haMadai amodeti lemachaziq ulema'oz lo: 'And in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to confirm and strengthen him.' The angelic speaker continues — this verse is the end of Gabriel's explanation, linking the heavenly guardian's role to the transition from Babylonian to Median-Persian rule. The heavenly figure's reinforcing activity occurs at the empire's transition, supporting the cosmic order behind the earthly political change.
2: ve'attah emet agid lach hinei od shloshah melachim omdim leParas: 'And now I will tell you the truth: Behold, three more kings shall arise in Persia.' The predictive schema is historically specific: the three kings after Cyrus are Cambyses, Pseudo-Smerdis/Bardiya, and Darius I (or Cambyses, Darius I, Xerxes depending on reckoning). The fourth who is richer than all — Xerxes I (486-465 BCE) — led the massive invasion of Greece (480 BCE). Yachaziq hakol et-malkhut Yavan (stirring up all against the kingdom of Greece): Xerxes' Persian Wars are the historical turning point that made Greece the successor power.
3: veamad melek gibor umashel mimshel rab ve'asah kirtsono: 'And a mighty king shall arise who shall rule with great dominion and do as he wills.' Gibor (H1368, mighty one/warrior): the term applied to Alexander the Great — the single most decisive military figure of the ancient world, who conquered the Persian empire in under a decade (336-323 BCE). Ve'asah kirtsono (did according to his will) — the language of absolute uncontested sovereignty used later for Antiochus IV (v.36) and the eschatological king. Alexander as the type of the self-willed conqueror is established here.
4: uveoamdo tishshaver malkhuto vetechelets le'arba ruchot hashamayim: 'And as soon as he has arisen, his kingdom shall be broken and divided toward the four winds of heaven.' The sudden death of Alexander (323 BCE) at age 32 and the immediate fragmentation of his empire into four kingdoms (the Diadochi — Ptolemy in Egypt, Seleucus in Syria/East, Cassander in Macedonia, Lysimachus in Thrace) is one of the most precisely fulfillable prophecies in the OT. Lo le'acharito velo kemoshlo asher mashal: not to his descendants, nor with authority like his — Alexander left no effective successor.
5: veyechezaq melek haNegev: 'The king of the south shall be strong.' Melek haNegev (king of the south): Ptolemy I Soter of Egypt (305-283 BCE). Melek haTzafon (king of the north): Seleucus I Nicator (305-281 BCE) who took control of the largest Diadochi fragment. The two competing dynasties — Ptolemies vs. Seleucids — will dominate the next hundred years of political history narrated in vv. 5-35, with the land of Israel caught between them as the geographic and strategic corridor.
6: uveketz shanim yitchaberu: 'After some years they shall make an alliance.' The diplomatic marriage between the two dynasties: Berenice daughter of Ptolemy II married Antiochus II Theos (the Seleucid king, vv. 6-7) ca. 252 BCE. Lo tachaziq koach hazroa (she shall not retain the strength of her arm): Berenice and her father and child were all murdered when Antiochus II restored his first wife Laodice (246 BCE). Political marriage as a covenant-mechanism is exposed here as fragile — human alliances cannot sustain themselves without deeper covenantal foundations.
7: veamad minetzur shorsheha al-kano: 'One from the branch of her roots shall arise in his place.' Ptolemy III Euergetes, Berenice's brother, succeeded his father (Ptolemy II) and invaded the Seleucid kingdom in revenge, penetrating as far as Seleucia-on-the-Tigris (the Third Syrian War, 246-241 BCE). The image of the branch/shoot from the royal root (netzur) echoes the Isaianic netzur imagery of the Davidic shoot (Isa 11:1 — a shoot from the stump of Jesse) — though here applied to a foreign king, not messianically.
8: vegam eloheihem im nasichehem im-keli chemdatam kesef vezahav yishbeh Mitsrayim: 'He shall also carry off to Egypt their gods with their metal images and their precious vessels of silver and gold.' Ptolemy III is said to have brought back to Egypt many cult statues previously taken to Babylon — the return of divine images was a powerful political-theological statement in the ancient Near East (cf. Cyrus returning the temple vessels to Jerusalem, Ezra 1:7-11). The gods themselves are carried as spoil, demonstrating the political victors' control over divine protection.
9: uva bemamlekhet melek haNegev veshuv el-admato: 'Then the latter shall come into the realm of the king of the south but shall return to his own land.' The mutual incursions between Ptolemaic and Seleucid kingdoms — each advancing, then retreating — mark the ongoing warfare. The cyclical nature of these conflicts, each leaving the fundamental power balance unchanged, reflects the vanity of military conquest apart from the divine decree that will eventually settle all earthly kingdoms.
10: uvanav yitgaru veasafu hamon chayalot rabbot: 'His sons shall wage war and assemble a multitude of great forces.' Antiochus III the Great (Seleucus III's successor) resumed the Syrian Wars, advancing through Palestine in the Fourth Syrian War (219-217 BCE) until defeated by Ptolemy IV at Raphia (217 BCE). Ve'avar veshataf ve'ashuv (he shall pass through, be swept away, and return) — the military flood-metaphor for advancing armies is the Isaianic image of the Assyrian invasion (Isa 8:7-8 — overflows and passes on).
11: veyitmarer melek haNegev: 'The king of the south shall be enraged.' Ptolemy IV's response to Antiochus III's advance — mobilizing a large force and winning at Raphia (217 BCE). Despite the large numbers mobilized, the victory did not consolidate Egyptian strength. Ki ya'amod hamon gadol: the great multitude is given into the hand of the victor, but the victory is incomplete — Ptolemy IV failed to capitalize strategically.
12: venasei hehamon yarumam vilvavo: 'When he has taken away the multitude, his heart shall be exalted.' Pride following military victory — the consistent biblical dynamic where success leads to hubris (cf. Deut 8:14; 2 Chr 26:16 on Uzziah). Ptolemy IV returned to Egypt after Raphia without pressing his advantage, and his reign declined. Vehippil rebebot velo ya'oz: he shall strike down tens of thousands yet shall not prevail — the decisive victory that fails to consolidate is a recurring historical pattern.
13: veshav melek haTzafon vehe'emid hamon rab: 'For the king of the north shall again raise a great multitude.' Antiochus III regrouped and, after Ptolemy IV's death (204 BCE) and the accession of the child Ptolemy V, renewed his campaign — the Fifth Syrian War (202-195 BCE), capturing Palestine. Uveketz ha'ittim shanim: after the appointed time — et (H6256) as the appointed/appropriate time, the same term used for YHWH's kairos moments throughout the prophets. History moves on YHWH's timeline, not human ambition.
14: uveat hahi rabbim yaqumu al-melek haNegev uvnei paritsei amcha yinasu lehakim chazun venichshalu: 'In those times many shall rise against the king of the south, and the violent ones among your own people shall lift themselves up in order to fulfill the vision, but they shall fail.' Bnei paritzei amcha (the violent ones of your people): Jewish pro-Seleucid factions who collaborated with Antiochus III, believing his conquest would fulfill the restoration prophecies. Their failure — venichshalu (they stumbled/fell) — illustrates the danger of presuming to force prophetic fulfillment through political-military action.
15: uveshavat ir hamivtzarot velo yaamdur zeroa haNegev: 'The king of the north shall come and cast up siege works and take a well-fortified city.' Sidon fell to Antiochus III in 198 BCE; the Egyptian general Scopas was defeated at Panion and besieged at Sidon. Zeroa (H2220, arm/strength): the military arm metaphor — Egypt's arm proved insufficient against the Seleucid advance. The arm/strength of earthly powers is consistently shown as inadequate in Daniel's vision.
16: veyaas haba elav kirtsono ve'ein oamed lefanav: 'He who comes against him shall do as he wills, and none shall stand before him.' Antiochus III's unchecked advance through Palestine after 198 BCE. Eretz haTzvi (the beautiful/glorious land): the standard prophetic designation for the land of Israel (Ezek 20:6, 15; Jer 3:19; Dan 8:9; 11:41) — the land that becomes the arena of competing imperial ambitions throughout vv. 5-35.
17: veyasem panav lavo bteqeph kol malkhuto: 'He shall set his face to come with the strength of his whole kingdom.' Antiochus III's diplomatic strategy — marrying his daughter Cleopatra I to the young Ptolemy V (193 BCE), hoping she would serve his interests in Egypt. Uvat hanashim yitennah lo lehashchitah: he shall give him a daughter of women to destroy him, but it shall not stand. Cleopatra I proved loyal to Egypt, not her father. The marriage-alliance strategy — using a daughter as a political weapon — illustrates the instrumentalization of persons in power politics, which Daniel's vision exposes as ultimately futile.
18: veyasev panav le'iyim ulakad rabbim: 'He shall turn his face to the coastlands and shall capture many of them.' Antiochus III's western campaigns (196-191 BCE) into Asia Minor and the Aegean islands, bringing him into conflict with Rome. Veheshiv lo cherpo (a commander shall put an end to his insolence): the Roman commander Lucius Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus defeated Antiochus III at Magnesia (190 BCE) — the first decisive Roman intervention in the eastern Mediterranean, signaling Rome's future imperial dominance.
19: veyasev panav lema'uzzei artzo venikhshal venapal velo yimmatze: 'Then he shall turn his face back toward the fortresses of his own land, but he shall stumble and fall, and shall not be found.' Antiochus III's humiliating retreat after Magnesia, his attempt to plunder a temple in Elymais to restore his depleted treasury (187 BCE), and his assassination there — the great Seleucid king ended obscurely. The language velo yimmatze (and shall not be found) suggests sudden disappearance from history's stage — a warning to all who trust in military strength.
20: veamad al-kanno over nogesh heder malkhut: 'In his place one shall arise who shall send an exactor of tribute through the glory of the kingdom.' Seleucus IV Philopator (187-175 BCE) who sent Heliodorus to plunder the Jerusalem temple treasury (2 Macc 3) — the historical background for the Maccabean crisis. Uvi'yamim achadim yishaber velo ve'aph beanaf velo bemilchamah: he shall be broken within a few days, neither in anger nor in war. Heliodorus poisoned Seleucus IV — an undramatic end that nevertheless transferred power to Antiochus IV.
21: veamad al-kanno niv'zeh velo natenu alav hod malkhut: 'In his place shall arise a contemptible person to whom royal majesty has not been given.' Niv'zeh (H959, despised/held in contempt): the same root as Isa 53:3's niv'zeh — the despised servant. Here it describes Antiochus IV Epiphanes who was not the legitimate heir (the throne belonged to Demetrius, son of Seleucus IV, held as a Roman hostage). Uvah beshilva vehecheziq malkhut bechalaqlaqot: he shall come in time of security and obtain the kingdom by flatteries — the seizure of power through intrigue rather than legitimate succession.
22: uzeroa hashtetef yishtof milfanav veyishaveru: 'Armies shall be utterly swept away before him and broken, even the prince of the covenant.' Nagid berit (prince of the covenant): debated — either a Ptolemaic official or the high priest Onias III, who was removed from office by Antiochus IV's intrigues (2 Macc 4:7-10). The desacralization of the high priesthood — nagid berit (covenant-prince) eliminated — marks the beginning of Antiochus's assault on the covenantal order.
23: ume'hitchabro elav ya'aseh mirmah: 'After an alliance is made with him he shall act deceitfully.' Mirmah (H4820, deceit/treachery): Antiochus IV's characteristic political mode. The Ptolemaic alliance he entered only to betray marks the corruption of covenant-making — the very instrument of international relations (treaties) is weaponized through deception.
24: beshilvah uvimshmane medinah yavo: 'In a time of security he shall come into the richest parts of the province.' Antiochus IV's innovative strategy of winning loyalty through lavish distribution of plunder — unlike his predecessors he gave away what he had seized, buying support. Veyachashor machashavot al-mivtzarim ve'ad et: he shall devise plans against strongholds, but only for a time. The et (appointed time) limit frames all his activities within divine permission — he acts within time granted, not unlimited.
25: veyaer kocho ulvavo al-melek haNegev bechayil gadol: 'He shall stir up his power and his heart against the king of the south with a great army.' Antiochus IV's first Egyptian campaign (170-169 BCE). Velo yaamood ki yachashvu alav machashavot: the Egyptian king shall not stand because plots shall be devised against him. The political betrayal by Egypt's own counselors mirrors the intrigue by which Antiochus himself seized power — the corruption spreads through all parties involved.
26: ve'ochelei patbago yishberuhu: 'The ones who eat his food shall break him.' The betrayal by court insiders — those who benefit from the king's table become the instruments of his downfall. The image of table-fellowship as the site of betrayal echoes the psalmist's lament (Ps 41:9 — the one who ate my bread lifted his heel against me) and anticipates the Judas-betrayal pattern. Inner circles are as dangerous as external enemies.
27: ushneihem hamelachim libbam lemira veal shulchan echad kazav yadaberu: 'And as for the two kings, their hearts shall be bent on doing evil. They shall speak lies at the same table.' The diplomatic fiction of negotiation as cover for mutual deception — both Antiochus IV and Ptolemy VI meet publicly as allies while planning against each other. Velo titzlach ki od qetz lamoedh: but it shall not succeed, for the end is yet to be at the time appointed. The divine moedh (appointed time) overrides all human plotting.
28: veyashov artzo birechush gadol ulvavo al-berit qodesh: 'And he shall return to his land with great wealth, but his heart shall be set against the holy covenant.' Antiochus IV's return from Egypt (169 BCE) with enormous plunder — including the pillaging of the Jerusalem temple (1 Macc 1:20-28; 2 Macc 5:15-21). Berit qodesh (holy covenant): the first explicit mention of the covenant as the target of Antiochus's hostility — not merely geopolitical rivalry but a theological-covenantal assault. The enemy of the people of God is ultimately the enemy of their covenant.
29: lamoedh yashuv uvah banegev velo tihyeh kharishon vekha'acharon: 'At the time appointed he shall return and come into the south, but it shall not be this time as it was before.' The second Egyptian campaign (168 BCE) was less successful — Antiochus IV was met at Eleusis by the Roman legate Gaius Popilius Laenas, who drew a circle in the sand around Antiochus and demanded he decide on peace before stepping out of the circle. Forced to withdraw, his frustration was redirected against Jerusalem.
30: uva bo tziyim Kittim venikh'ah veshav veza'am al-berit qodesh: 'For ships of Kittim shall come against him, and he shall be afraid and withdraw, and shall turn back and be enraged and take action against the holy covenant.' Kittim (H3794): originally Cypress, later used for western maritime powers — here Rome. The Roman humiliation at Eleusis is the turning point: unable to vent his fury on Rome, Antiochus redirected against the covenant people. Veshav veham al-ozvei berit qodesh: he shall pay attention to those who forsake the holy covenant — the Hellenizing Jewish collaborators who aided his assault on Judaism.
31: uzeroa mimenu yaamodu vechill'lu et-haMiqdash haMa'oz veheisiru hatamid venatenu hashiquts meshomem: 'Forces from him shall appear and profane the temple and fortress, and shall take away the regular burnt offering and set up the abomination that makes desolate.' Hasorim hatamid (removing the regular/continual offering): Antiochus IV abolished the daily tamid sacrifice in Kislev 167 BCE (1 Macc 1:39, 45). Shiquts meshomem (abomination of desolation): the altar of Zeus Olympios erected on the altar of burnt offering (1 Macc 1:54; 2 Macc 6:2) — Jesus identifies this as the signal for the eschatological tribulation (Mark 13:14).
32: umarishiei berit yachanif bechalaqlaqot veyodei Elohav yacharziqu veasu: 'He shall seduce with flattery those who violate the covenant, but the people who know their God shall stand firm and take action.' Yodei Eloheihem (those who know their God): yada (H3045, know) as covenantal intimacy and experiential relationship — not merely intellectual knowledge but the deep personal knowledge of a people in covenant. Yacharziqu veasu: they shall stand firm and act — the combination of interior resolve and exterior witness marks the Maccabean faithful as the paradigm for covenant loyalty under pressure.
33: umaskilei am yavinu larabim venikhshalu beherev uvelehavah bishvi uvevizah yamim: 'And the wise among the people shall make many understand, though for some days they shall stumble by sword and flame, by captivity and plunder.' Maskilei am (those who make wise among the people): maskil from sakal (H7919, be prudent/give insight) — the teacher-sages who help the community understand what is happening in light of covenant history. Their ministry costs them severely — sword, flame, captivity, plunder. The teacher of truth in a hostile empire bears the cost of fidelity.
34: ubehibashalam yee'zeru ezra me'at venitlavu aleihem rabbim bachalaqlaqot: 'When they stumble, they shall receive a little help. And many shall join themselves to them with flattery.' Ezra me'at (a little help): the Maccabean revolt (begun 167 BCE) is described with striking understatement — not the glorious deliverance but only modest assistance, with opportunists joining insincerely. The phrase bachalaqlaqot (with flatteries) — the same root used for Antiochus's intrigue (v.21) now characterizing those who attach to the faithful cause for self-protection.
35: umin hamaskilim yikashlu letzaref bahem ulvarer ullilaben ad-et qetz: 'And some of the wise shall stumble, so that they may be refined, purified, and made white, until the time of the end, for it is yet for the time appointed.' The suffering of the righteous teachers is explicitly purposive: letzaref (to refine like metal), levarer (to purify), lillaben (to make white). Three metallurgical-laundering metaphors for the purification that persecution produces (cf. Mal 3:3; 1 Pet 1:7). Ki od lamo'ed: the suffering is bounded — the appointed time limits all persecution.
36: veyitromem veyitgadal al-kol-El veal El Elim yedabber niflaot: 'And the king shall do as he wills. He shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god, and shall speak astonishing things against the God of gods.' Yitromem veyitgadal: both Hitpael (reflexive) verbs — self-exaltation doubled. Niflaot (H6381, wonders/monstrous things/things beyond capacity): the claim to divine status and power beyond what any human can legitimately claim. This verse transitions from the historical Antiochus IV to what many interpreters read as a typological figure — the eschatological self-divinizing power (cf. 2 Thess 2:3-4; Rev 13:5-6) who recapitulates Antiochus's pattern at history's end.
37: veal Elohei avotav lo yavin veal chemdath nashim: 'He shall pay no attention to the gods of his fathers, or to the one beloved by women.' Elohei avotav (gods of his fathers): abandoning ancestral religion — Antiochus IV adopted the cult of Zeus Olympios and demanded divine worship for himself. Chemdath nashim (desire/beloved of women): the most natural reading is Adonis/Tammuz, the dying-and-rising deity widely mourned by women (cf. Ezek 8:14 — women weeping for Tammuz). Antiochus's religious innovations eliminated even this popular cult. The eschatological king abandons all prior religious loyalties in pursuit of absolute self-divinization.
38: veal-Elohei ma'uzim al kanno yechabbed: 'He shall honor the god of fortresses instead of these.' Elohei ma'uzim (god of fortresses/strongholds): the deity of military power — possibly Mars/Ares or Jupiter Capitolinus, or simply the raw power of military force. The eschatological king worships raw power — not as means to an end but as ultimate value. This is the logic of empire stripped of its religious veneer: the only god that matters is the god who gives military victory.
39: ve'asah lemivatsei ma'uzim im-elohah nechar: 'He shall deal with the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god.' The combination of military strategy and religious innovation — using new religious incentives (yarbeh kavod, increase honor) to motivate soldiers. Vehimshilan barabim vechamat adamah yechalek bishcar: dividing the land as a bribe. The monetization of loyalty through territorial grants marks the transactional politics of totalitarian power.
40: ubeet qetz yitnagach imo melek haNegev: 'At the time of the end, the king of the south shall attack him.' The et qetz (time of the end) marks the transition to eschatological horizon — whether continuing the Antiochus IV narrative or moving to a future figure who recapitulates his pattern. The military flood-metaphor resumes: yishtatef ba'aratzot (overflow through many countries) — the Assyrian/Babylonian flood-image (Isa 8:7-8; Dan 9:26) applied to the final military campaign.
41: uvoa b'Eretz haTzvi verobot yikashelu: 'He shall come into the beautiful land, and tens of thousands shall fall.' Eretz haTzvi (the beautiful/desirable land) recurs as the focal point — the land of covenant, which becomes the eschatological battle-ground. Ve'eleh yimatlu miyado Edom uMo'av ureishit Bnei Ammon: Edom, Moab, and the firstfruits of Ammon escape — traditional Israel-enemies who are preserved, paradoxically, when Israel suffers. The selectivity of eschatological judgment preserves historical irony.
42: viyishllach yado ba'aratzot uEretz Mitsrayim lo tihyeh liphletah: 'He shall stretch out his hand against the countries, and the land of Egypt shall not escape.' Shalach yad (stretch out the hand) is the Exodus-language of YHWH's powerful intervention (Exod 3:20; 7:5) — the eschatological conqueror appropriates the Exodus gesture of divine action for his own military campaign, the ultimate parody of divine power.
43: umashol bemiqdanot hazahav vekheseph uvechol chamudot Mitsrayim: 'He shall become ruler of the treasures of gold and of silver, and all the precious things of Egypt.' The wealth of empires — gold, silver, precious things — that Daniel's vision catalogs through eleven chapters is ultimately exposed as transient. The rich nations that trusted their treasuries are stripped. Valuvim uKushim bimitzadav: the Libyans and Cushites following in his train — the widest geographic extent of the final empire's dominion, reaching Egypt and its African dependencies.
44: vishmuot yebahaluhu mizrach utzafon veyatze bechemah gedolah: 'But news from the east and the north shall alarm him, and he shall go out with great fury to destroy and devote many to destruction.' The reversal: at the moment of maximum expansion, alarming news from flank regions creates the conditions for the conqueror's undoing. Lehashmid ulehachriem rabbim (to destroy and devote many to destruction): the cherem (H2763, sacred destruction/ban) language of holy war — the total-destruction devoted to the deity. The final military campaign employs the vocabulary of sacred destruction, the ultimate parody of covenant warfare.
45: veyita oholei apadno bein yamim lehar tzevi qodesh: 'And he shall pitch his palatial tents between the sea and the glorious holy mountain.' The eschatological conqueror establishes his camp between the Mediterranean and the Temple Mount — the geographically and theologically central location of the cosmos in biblical geography. Uvah ad-qitzo ve'ein ozer lo: 'Yet he shall come to his end, with none to help him.' The three-word oracle — qitzo ve'ein ozer — is the anti-climax of the entire chapter's imperial narrative: the self-divinizing conqueror dies without help, alone, the way every absolute power ends. YHWH's moedh (appointed time) has the last word.
